![image 1](data:image/png;base64,)

![image 2](data:image/png;base64,)

# Research and Applications

# Evaluating algorithmic bias on biomarker classification of breast cancer pathology reports

Jordan Tschida , PhD 1, � , Mayanka Chandrashekar , PhD 1 , Alina Peluso, PhD 1 , Zachary Fox, PhD 1 , Patrycja Krawczuk, MS 1 , Dakota Murdock, BS 1 , Xiao-Cheng Wu , PhD 2 , John Gounley, PhD 1 , Heidi A. Hanson, PhD 1

1 2

Advanced Computing for Health Sciences, Oak Ridge National Laboratory, Oak Ridge, TN 37830, United States, Department of Epidemiology, Louisiana State University New Orleans School of Public Health, New Orleans, LA 70112, United States � Corresponding author: Jordan Tschida, PhD, Advanced Computing for Health Sciences, Oak Ridge National Laboratory, 5700 1 Bethel Valley Road, Oak Ridge, TN 37830, United States (tschidajl@ornl.gov)

# Abstract

Objectives: This work evaluated algorithmic bias in biomarkers classification using electronic pathology reports from female breast cancer cases. Bias was assessed across 5 subgroups: cancer registry, race, Hispanic ethnicity, age at diagnosis, and socioeconomic status.

Materials and Methods: We utilized 594 875 electronic pathology reports from 178 121 tumors diagnosed in Kentucky, Louisiana, New Jersey, New Mexico, Seattle, and Utah to train 2 deep-learning algorithms to classify breast cancer patients using their biomarkers test results. We used balanced error rate (BER), demographic parity (DP), equalized odds (EOD), and equal opportunity (EOP) to assess bias.

Results: We found differences in predictive accuracy between registries, with the highest accuracy in the registry that contributed the most data (Seattle Registry, BER ratios for all registries > 1.25). BER showed no significant algorithmic bias in extracting biomarkers (estrogen receptor, progesterone receptor, human epidermal growth factor receptor 2) for race, Hispanic ethnicity, age at diagnosis, or socioeconomic subgroups (BER ratio < 1.25). DP, EOD, and EOP all showed insignificant results.

Discussion: We observed significant differences in BER by registry, but no significant bias using the DP, EOD, and EOP metrics for sociodemographic or racial categories. This highlights the importance of employing a diverse set of metrics for a comprehensive evaluation of model fairness.

Conclusion: A thorough evaluation of algorithmic biases that may affect equality in clinical care is a critical step before deploying algorithms in the real world. We found little evidence of algorithmic bias in our biomarker classification tool. Artificial intelligence tools to expedite information extraction from clinical records could accelerate clinical trial matching and improve care.

# Lay Summary

Breast cancer subtypes are determined by molecular indicators of disease or biomarkers. Humans manually code pathology reports that detail biomarker status. The use of artificial intelligence (AI) to automate this process is being explored by the National Cancer Institute SEER registries. If unchecked, the use of AI could increase health inequality if the performance of the algorithm is dependent upon the patient's characteristics. This is known as algorithmic bias. Algorithmic biases may affect equality in clinical care; therefore, it is a critical step before deployment to ensure fair and  equitable  performance  across  demographics.  Based  on  this  concern,  we  used  machine  learning  and  bias  metrics  to  determine  if  our population-level cancer surveillance data for biomarker classification was biased. We evaluated algorithmic bias across 5 subgroups: cancer registry, race, Hispanic ethnicity, age at diagnosis, and socioeconomic status. We found little evidence of bias in our biomarker classification algorithm.

Key words: population-level; algorithmic bias; artificial intelligence; breast cancer; biomarkers.

# Background

Breast cancer is the most common cancer diagnosis and is the second-leading  cause  of  death  for  women  in  the  United States. Biomarker testing, immunohistochemistry, and/or fluorescence  in  situ  hybridization  is  commonly  used  by  clinicians to develop a prognosis and  determine treatment strategies for breast cancer patients. 1,2 These tests reveal the status of the 3 biomarkers-estrogen receptor (ER), progesterone  receptor  (PR),  and  human  epidermal  growth  factor receptor 2 (HER2)-and categorize tumors as positive , negative , or equivocal . Tumors can be further classified by combining  information  across  all  3  biomarkers.  Triple-negative breast  cancer  (TNBC)-which  is  negative  for  ER,  PR,  and HER2-is notable  for  being  more  aggressive  with  elevated mortality rates.

There are known racial and sociodemographic disparities in breast cancer incidence and mortality that are also closely associated  with  disparities  in  access  and  utilization  of  biomarker testing. White women have the highest breast cancer incidence rates, followed by Black, Native American, Asian and Pacific Islander, and then Hispanic women. 3 However, Black  women  have  a  40%  higher  mortality  rate  for  breast cancer  than  White  women. 4,5 Black  women  are  also  more likely to be diagnosed with TNBC. 6

Individuals with lower income and less education have a higher  breast  cancer  mortality  rate  and  lower  odds  of  biomarker testing compared to those with a higher income and more education. 7-9 Women younger than 45 who are diagnosed with breast cancer have a decreased survival rate, more aggressive tumors, and are more likely to be diagnoses with a TNBC. 10-14

Accurately  extracting  biomarker  results  from  electronic health  records  can  improve  clinical  decision-making  and accelerate clinical trial matching. However, if an algorithmic bias exists, these tools may widen known disparities in breast cancer  mortality.  To  date,  understanding  the  ethical  challenges in machine  learning (ML)  models  is an underresearched  area 15 ;  therefore,  an  in-depth  investigation  into these potential issues is essential for algorithms deployed for precision  medicine  or  precision  population  health.  To  our knowledge, we are the first study to examine algorithm bias in biomarker prediction tools.

This study leverages ML and bias metrics on populationlevel cancer surveillance data for biomarker classification to measure algorithmic bias. Algorithmic bias, defined here as the discrepancy in model performance perpetuated from representation  bias  in  the  data, 16 is  a  key  focus  of  this  work, given  its  potential  implications  for  exacerbating  healthcare disparities.  We  then  assess  algorithmic  bias  across  5  subgroups  (registry,  racial  category,  Hispanic  ethnicity,  age  at diagnosis, and socioeconomic status [SES]) to uncover underlying trends that could influence model performance.

# Materials and methods

# Data source

This study utilized data from 594 875 female breast cancer epath reports for 178 121 breast cancer diagnoses from 2011 to 2021 obtained from the Kentucky, Louisiana, New Jersey, New  Mexico,  Seattle,  and  Utah  SEER  cancer  registries through the institutional review board protocol DOE000152. Each  report  provided  comprehensive  details,  including  the date created, unique patient and tumor identifiers, and diagnostic  information recorded by the pathologist. The pathological information generally contains test results for cancer biomarkers,  including  ER,  PR,  and  HER2.  Gold-standard labels were derived from the Collaborative Stage Site-Specific Factors variables manually coded by cancer registrars, classified  as Negative , Positive ,  or Unknown for  ER,  PR,  and HER2.  Detailed  information  about  the  classification  rules can be found in Section 'Data definitions' and Supplementary Materials A.

# Study design

Our  approach  follows  a  2-step  classification  pipeline:  (1) extract  biomarker  information  from  pathology  reports  and (2)  aggregate  this  information  at  the  tumor  level  for  final classification.

We  used  the  Batch-processing  Abstraction  for  Raw  Data Integration  for  text  cleaning  and  normalization  outlined  in Supplementary Materials B. We tested 2 deep learning models for the classification task; MTCNN and MTHisan. We found that for this task, MTCNN had a higher accuracy level than our commonly used Hierarchical Self-Attention Neural Network (HiSAN) model 17 ; refer to Supplementary Materials C, Table 1. Patients often have more than one report describing their  clinical  test  values.  We  apply  the  Case-Level  Context

(CLC) model developed by Gao et al 18 to classify all reports for a given tumor, ensuring the use of the full patient history for classification.

A detailed explanation of data handling is provided in Figure 1. Importantly, the MTCNN and CLC model architectures are open  source and accessible via  the  FrESCO framework. 19

Data were randomly split into training (70%), validation (15%), and testing (15%) sets. This resulted in a final test set composed  of  89  282  e-path  reports  and  26  728  tumors. Because TNBC is associated with disparities in diagnosis and outcomes, we assessed algorithmic bias by relabeling the data into 5 subgroups (see Section 'Data definitions'). This relabeling process enabled us to evaluate the model's accuracy in identifying TNBC tumors, a crucial factor given the impact of  this  classification  on  patient  care.  Table  1 provides  a detailed breakdown of the dataset. Tumor counts were determined by concatenating e-paths with a unique patient ID and a unique tumor ID for each registry.

# Data definitions

Tumor Classification Labels: To assess TNBC classification accuracy, we derived a binary label from ER, PR, and HER2 predictions. The model architecture learns multilabel classification (ie, HER2, ER, PR), in which each label is multiclass (negative,  positive,  unknown).  ER,  PR,  and  HER2  predictions  were  combined  to  create  a  binary  classification  for TNBC, which is thought to play a role in racial differences in breast cancer mortality. If the tumor was negative for all 3 biomarkers,  then  reports  were  labeled  as TNBC .  Reports positive  for  ER,  PR,  or  HER2  were  labeled  as non-TNBC , and reports not matching the above criteria were labeled as unknown . Refer to Table 2 in Supplementary Materials D for supplemental details on implementation.

To assess fairness, we analyzed model performance across several  key  demographic and clinical subgroups, defined as follows.

Registry: Since  diagnostic  practices  vary  by  region,  we examined  model  accuracy  across  SEER  registries.  Regional differences  in  pathological  reporting  standards,  laboratory practices,  and  interpretation  guidelines  across  institutions and healthcare systems may lead to differences in model accuracy in the SEER registry, 20 namely for Kentucky, Louisiana, New Jersey, New Mexico, Seattle, or Utah. The evaluation of regional differences in model accuracy involved 2 key aspects. First,  we  analyzed  variability  in  the  model's  performance across  registries.  Second,  we  assessed  accuracy  differences between Black and White women within each registry.

Race: Achieving equitable model performance on our data holds particular significance for TNBC because Black women are more prone to this subtype of breast cancer. 6 We follow the standard set in the 2011 SEER registry manual 21 and use the  broad  race  categories  of  White,  Black,  Pacific  Islander, Native American, Asian, Other, and Unknown. This approach enables us to assess equitable outcomes across each race category. 7,8,22 We assess racial bias in 2 ways. First, we assess racial bias in model performance independent of other variables. Second, we assess racial bias for each of the tumor classes.

Hispanic ethnicity: People of Hispanic ethnicity experience known  disparities  in  healthcare  access,  higher  reliance  on public  healthcare  systems,  and  lower  SES. 3,7,23 Unequal model  performance  for  this  group  could  serve  to  widen

|Black|69 671 (12%)|20 177 (11%)|4105 (20%)|15 441 (77%)|631 (3%)|
|Native America|3425 (0.6%)|1133 (0.6%)|117 (10%)|977 (86%)|39 (3%)|
|Asian|24 002 (4%)|7381 (4%)|652 (9%)|6591 (89%)|138 (2%)|
|Pacific Islander|1784 (0.3%)|584 (0.3%)|45 (8%)|527 (90%)|12 (2%)|
|Other a|1618 (0.3%)|483 (0.3%)|54 (11%)|401 (83%)|28 (6%)|
|Unknown b|4531 (0.8%)|1848 (1%)|153 (8%)|1461 (79%)|234 (13%)|
|Hispanic origin| | | | | |

|Hispanic|43 425 (7%)|12 548 (7%)|1399 (11%)|10 661 (85%)|488 (4%)|
|Age at diagnosis| | | | | |
|Under 44 c|56 787 (10%)|15 947 (9%)|2527 (16%)|13 053 (82%)|368 (2%)|
|Ages 45-70|370 730 (63%)|108 545 (61%)|11 555 (11%)|94 375 (87%)|2615 (2%)|
|71 or older|165 117 (28%)|53 626 (30%)|4737 (9%)|47 315 (88%)|1574 (3%)|

|Lower 25%|68 746 (12%)|20 613 (12%)|2750 (13%)|17 180 (83%)|683 (3%)|

- a SEER coded when race is something other than the ones listed.
- b SEER coded when race is unknown.


c Includes < 22 age group; total e-path and tumor percentages are calculated column-wise: total subgroup divided by the total. TNBC, non-TNBC, and unknown tumor column percentages are calculated row-wise: total tumor type for the subgroup divided by the total tumors for the subgroup.

healthcare disparities. Based on SEER registry reporting, this subgroup comprises 2 discrete categories: non-Hispanic and Hispanic.

Age at diagnosis: Although rare, breast cancer incidence in women younger than 45 indicates more aggressive forms of cancer. 3,6,10-13 If model performance were to vary by age at diagnosis,  important  classifications  in  younger  age  groups could  be  missed  because  they  have  fewer  data  points.  We included an auxiliary group of women under the age of 22 because assessing model performance in these infrequent yet clinically significant cases is beneficial to clinical ML applications.  This  subgroup  was  analogous  with  mammography screening ages of < 22, 22-44, 45-70, and 71 þ .

Socioeconomic status: SES is linked to breast cancer outcomes  and  mortality;  disparate  model  accuracy  across  SES classes could serve to widen these gaps. 24-26 County of diagnosis was linked to the 2020 Census data, and county-level Median Family Income was used to measure SES. The income estimate  data  are  from  the  US  Department  of  Commerce, Bureau of the Census, Small Area Income and Poverty Estimates Program. 27 Tumors were classified by patients living in a county in the bottom 25%, middle 50%, and top 25% of the median family income distribution.

# Statistical analysis

To ensure both high overall accuracy and equitable performance  across  demographic  groups,  we  evaluated  our  models using both standard ML metrics and fairness metrics.

We utilized  existing  ML  metrics  and  bias  metrics  in  our evaluation  of  algorithmic  bias  to  provide  a  comprehensive assessment  of  the  model's  performance.  ML  metrics  (ie, micro-F1,  macro-F1,  and  precision)  measure  how  well  the deep-learning  model  is  learning  the  population-level  data, providing insight into how this data can create generalizable models. Bias metrics (ie, demographic parity [DP], equalized odds [EOD], and equal opportunity [EOP]) assess the model's performance on various demographic groups within this population-level data to ensure equitable model performance across these demographics. Refer to Supplementary Materials E for more details on implementation.

# Results

Refer to Table 2 for micro F1s, macro F1s, precision, recall, balanced error rate (BER) ratios, DP ratios, EOP ratios, and EOD ratios  for  all  5  subgroups.  The  bold  numbers  in  the table represent the highest accuracy for a given group.

Registry: We  found  a  difference  of  0.09  in  F1  values between the  registries  with  the  lowest  accuracy  (Louisiana, micro  F1 ¼ 0.86)  and  the  highest  accuracy  (Seattle,  micro F1 ¼ 0.95). The discrepancies were even larger when considering  the  macro  F1  (New  Jersey,  macro  F1 ¼ 0.55;  Seattle, macro F1 ¼ 0.71). There was a difference of 0.18 in recall values between the registry with the highest precision (Seattle, recall ¼ 0.83) and the registry with the lowest precision (Louisiana, recall ¼ 0.65). There was a difference of 0.13 in recall values between registry with the highest recall (Seattle, 0.67) and  registry  with  the  lowest  recall  (New  Jersey  and  Utah, 0.54).

Seattle constituted the majority class, accounting for 26% of  the  data.  The  BER  ratio  results  were  between  1.29  and 1.53  for  all  registries  and  demonstrated  significant  bias toward data from the Seattle Registry ( > 1.25). The BER ratio

Figure 1. Flowchart of the data processing pipeline.

scores suggest biomarker reporting had a high error rate and that  the  model  favors  the  registries  with  the  most  training data. DP, EOD, and EOP scores were insignificant and suggest no bias in the model classification by registry (DP ¼ 0.97-1.00; EOD ¼ 0.10-0.16; EOP ¼ 0.09-0.13).

Racial differences: We found a difference of 0.13 in F1 values between the races with the lowest accuracy (Other micro F1 ¼ 0.82) and the highest accuracy (Pacific Islander women micro F1 ¼ 0.95). The discrepancies were less when considering the macro F1 (Pacific Islander women, macro f1 ¼ 0.63; Other women, macro f1 ¼ 0.55).  There  was  a  difference  of 0.30 in precision values between the races with the highest precision (Pacific Islander women, precision ¼ 0.80) and the lowest precision (Other women, precision ¼ 0.50). There was a difference of 0.09 in recall values between the race with the highest  recall  (Pacific  Islander,  recall ¼ 0.66)  and  the  race with the lowest recall (Asian recall, 0.56).

White  women  constituted  the  majority  class,  accounting for 82% of the data. Although the BER ratio showed insignificant  results  for  Black  women  (1.14),  Native  American women (BER ¼ 1.05), Asian women (BER ¼ 1.05), and Other women (BER ¼ 1.05), the BER was greater than 1.0, which indicates a model preference for the majority class. In contrast, the model exhibited significant BER bias toward Pacific Islanders (BER ¼ 0.77). Owing to the small sample size, the interpretation  of  these  findings  should  not  be  given  much weight. DP, EOD, and EOP scores were insignificant and suggest no bias in the model classification by race (DP ¼ 0.951.01; EOD ¼ 0.00-0.10; EOP ¼ 0.01-0.08).

Black  and  White  racial  differences  by  registry: For  the TNBC tumor class, we found Black women with cancer had at least a difference of 0.05 or larger (up to 66) in micro F1 score  compared  with  White  women  among  the  Kentucky, Louisiana, New Jersey, Seattle, and Utah registries; however, in  New Mexico, White women had a difference of 0.11 in micro F1 compared to Black for TNBC. For the non-TNBC tumor class, the micro F1 for White women with cancer had a  maximum difference  of  0.07  compared  to  Black  women. We found the largest difference for the unknown tumor class to be 0.35 for Black women in New Mexico and the smallest difference of 0.02 between Black and White women in Louisiana  in  micro  F1.  White  women  were  the  majority  class,

Highest accuracies in bold. BER ratio: < 0.8 ¼ significant in favor of Minority Class; 0.8-1.25 Insignificant Results; 1.25 ¼ Significant in favor of Majority Class. Demographic Parity Ratio: Results < 0.80 are Significant. Equal Opportunity and Equal Odds: Results > 0.25 are Significant; < 22 was removed due to the low sample size

a Majority class.

� Results greater than 1 but less than 1.25 demonstrate a preference for the majority group.

� Indicates statistically significant results.

Abbreviation: CI, confidence interval.

accounting for 82% of the data. Given results for DP, EOD, and EOP in races did not show significant bias, we only evaluated bias using BER for Black and White women by registry. The BER ratios showed insignificant differences among Kentucky  (BER ¼ 1.00),  Louisiana  (BER ¼ 1.08),  New  Jersey (BER ¼ 1.00), Seattle (BER ¼ 1.06), and Utah (BER ¼ 0.108) registries  but  still  demonstrated  a  model  preference  for  the majority  class.  New  Mexico  (BER ¼ 0.70)  was  significantly more  biased  toward  Black  women  than  White  women. Table 3 lists the Black and White women's racial differences by registry. Numbers listed in bold represent the highest accuracy of a given group.

Racial differences by tumor class: Black women constitute 20% of TNBC cases in our dataset compared to 8%-11% for  other  racial  groups.  We  found  a  difference  of  0.29  in accuracy between the TNBC racial tumor class with the lowest  accuracy  (Pacific  Islanders,  micro  F1 ¼ 0.92)  and  the TNBC racial  tumor  class  with  the  highest  accuracy  (Asian women, micro F1 ¼ 0.63). We found a difference of 0.09 in accuracy between the non-TNBC racial tumor class between races  (Pacific  Islander  women,  micro  f1 ¼ 0.98;  Black  and Other  women,  micro  f1 ¼ 0.89).  We  found  a  difference  of 0.29 in accuracy between the TNBC racial tumor classes with the lowest accuracy (Native American women, micro F1 ¼ 0.29%) and the TNBC racial tumor class with the highest  accuracy  (Pacific  Islander  and  Other  women,  micro F1 ¼ 0.00%). Table 4 lists racial differences by tumor classes. Numbers listed in bold represent the highest accuracy  of  a given group. For each tumor class, we see comparable model accuracies across all races. Given results for DP, EOD, and EOP in races did not show significant bias, we only evaluated bias using BER for racial differences by tumor class. These results  demonstrate  that  the  model  is  learning  tumor  classlevel features.

Hispanic ethnicity: We found a difference of 0.03 in accuracy between the ethnicity with the lower accuracy (Hispanic women, micro F1 ¼ 0.87%) and the ethnicity with the higher accuracy (non-Hispanic women, micro F1 ¼ 0.96%). The discrepancies were larger when considering the Macro F1 (nonHispanic women, 0.64; Hispanic women, 0.57). We found a difference of 0.07 between the ethnicity with the higher precision (Hispanic women precision ¼ 0.66%) and the lower precision (non-Hispanic women precision ¼ 0.59%). There was a  difference  of  0.04  in  recall  (Hispanic,  recall ¼ 0.58; NonHispanic,  recall ¼ 0.54).  Non-Hispanic  women  represented the majority class, accounting for 93% of the data. The BER, DP,  EOD,  and  EOP  results  were  insignificant  and  did  not demonstrate  bias  toward  the  majority  class  (BER ¼ 1.14; DP ¼ 0.99; EOD ¼ 0.04; EOP ¼ 0.05). This suggests that biomarker reporting does not vary by Hispanic ethnicity.

Age at diagnosis: We found a difference of 0.14 in accuracy between  the  age  group  with  the  lowest  accuracy  (younger than 22, micro F1 ¼ 0.78) and the age group with the highest accuracy (71 þ , micro F1 ¼ 0.92). The discrepancies were the same when considering the Macro F1 (71 þ , macro f1 ¼ 0.65; younger than 22, macro f1 ¼ 0.51). There was a difference of 0.06 in the age group with the lowest precision (under 22 precision ¼ 0.62%) and the age group with the highest precision

|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| |F1|N ¼ tumors|CI|F1|N ¼ tumors|CI|F1|N ¼ tumors|CI| | |
|White a|0.64|466|(0.60, 0.68)|0.95|3755|Kentucky (0.94, 0.96)|0.26|70|(0.16, 0.36)|0.22|-|
|Black|0.73|75|(0.63, 0.83)|0.91|256|(0.87, 0.95) Louisiana|0.29|< 10|(-, -)|0.22|1.0|
|White a|0.54|299|(0.48, 0.60)|0.94|2796|(0.93, 0.95)|0.19|95|(0.11, 0.27)|0.25|-|
|Black|0.63|360|(0.58, 0.68)|0.87|1076|(0.85, 0.89)|0.17|57|(0.07, 0.27)|0.27|1.08 b|
|White a|0.61|489|(0.57, 0.65)|0.95|4710|New Jersey (0.94, 0.96)|0.09|133|(0.04, 0.14)|0.25|-|
|Black|0.66|154|(0.59, 0.73)|0.92|672|(0.90, 0.94)|0.06|33|(-0.02, 0.14)|0.25|1.00|
|White a|0.68|128|(0.60, 0.76)|0.93|1174|New Mexico (0.92, 0.94)|0.15|88|(0.08, 0.22)|0.23|-|
|Black|0.57|< 10|(-, -)|0.92|19|(0.8, 1.04)|0.50|< 10|(-, -)|0.16|0.70 c|
|White a|0.79|462|(0.75, 0.83)|0.98|5144|Seattle (0.98, 0.98)|0.34|89|(0.24, 0.44)|0.17|-|
|Black|0.95|51|(0.89, 1.00)|0.97|178|(0.94, 1.00) Utah|0.00|< 10|(-, -)|0.18|1.06 b|
|White a|0.66|163|(0.59, 0.73)|0.97|1966|(0.96, 0.98)|0.08|22|(-0.03, 0.19)|0.24|-|
|Black|0.00|< 10|(-, -)|0.98|20|(0.92, 1.00)|0.00|< 10|(-, -)|0.26|1.08 b|


a Majority class. Highest accuracies are marked in bold . CIs were not calculated for samples Tumors ¼ < 10 due to having too small of data to accurately calculate scores. To avoid inaccurate CI scores, we did not compute them for groups with less than 10 samples.

- b Results greater than 1 but less than 1.25 demonstrate a preference for the majority group.
- c Significant results.


Abbreviation: CI, confidence interval.

Table 4. BER scores by race and tumor class for all registries.

| |All registries| | | | | | | | | | |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| |TNBC| | |Non-TNBC| | |Unknown| | |BER|Ratio|
| |F1|N ¼ tumors|CI|F1|N ¼ tumors|CI|F1|N ¼ tumors|CI| | |
|White c|0.66|2007|(0.64, 0.68)|0.96|19 545|(0.96, 0.96)|0.19|497|(0.16, 0.22)|0.22|-|
|Black|0.67|642|(0.63, 0.71)|0.90|2221|(0.89, 0.91)|0.15|105|(0.08, 0.22)|0.25|1.14 a|
|Native American|0.69|19|(0.48, 0.90)|0.93|150|(0.89, 0.97)|0.29|11|(0.02, 0.56)|0.23|1.05 a|
|Asian|0.63|87|(0.53, 0.73)|0.96|970|(0.95, 0.97)|0.15|21|(0.0, 0.3)|0.23|1.05 a|
|Pacific Islander|0.92|< 10|(-, -)|0.98|87|(-, -)|0.00|< 10|(-, -)|0.17|0.77 b|
|Other|0.75|< 10|(-, -)|0.89|53|(0.81, 0.97)|0.00|< 10|(-, -)|0.24|1.09 a|
|Unknown|0.78|22|(0.61, 0.95)|0.92|227|(0.88, 0.96)|0.18|40|(0.06, 0.3)|0.21|0.95|


Highest accuracies are marked in bold . CIs were not calculated for samples Tumors ¼ < 10 due to having too small of data to accurately calculate scores. To avoid inaccurate CI scores, we did not compute them for groups with less than 10 samples.

- a Results greater than 1 but less than 1.25 demonstrate a preference for the majority group.
- b Significant results.
- c Majority Class.


Abbreviation: CI, confidence interval.

(71 þ precision ¼ 0.68%). There was a difference of 0.03 in recall between the age group with the highest recall (22-44, recall ¼ 0.60) and the age group with the lowest recall (4570,  recall ¼ 0.57).  The  45-70  age  group  constituted  the majority, accounting for 61% of the data. We do not report results from the group younger than 22 due to the low sample size.  EOP  results  were  insignificant  and  suggest  biomarker reporting does not vary by age group (EOP ¼ 0.01-0.07).

Median family income: We found a difference of 0.07 in accuracy  between  the  SES  group  with  the  lowest  accuracy (lower  25%,  micro  F1 ¼ 0.86)  and  the  SES  group  with  the highest accuracy (upper 25%, micro F1 ¼ 0.93). The discrepancies  were  lower  when  considering  the  Macro  F1  (upper 25%, macro f1 ¼ 0.64; lower 25%, macro f1 ¼ 0.60). When considering  the  precision,  there  was  a  difference  of  0.06 (upper 25%, precision ¼ 0.67%, lower 25%, precision ¼ 0.61%). When  considering recall, there was a difference  of  0.05  (upper  25%,  recall ¼ 0.59;  lower  25%, recall ¼ 0.54).  The  upper  25%  constituted  the  majority group, accounting for 58% of the data. The BER, DP, EOD, and  EOP  results  showed  no  bias  between  the  lower  25% (BER ¼ 1.15; DP ¼ 0.98; EOD ¼ 0.07; EOP ¼ 0.05) and middle 50% (BER ¼ 1.10; DP ¼ 0.99; EOD ¼ 0.03; EOP ¼ 0.02). This suggests that biomarker reporting does not vary by SES.

# Discussion

Utilizing data collected by 6 cancer registries between 2011 and 2021, we trained an algorithm to extract HER2, ER, and PR test results for 178 121 tumors. Although SEER data collection  captures  nearly  all  cancer  cases  within  a  registry catchment area, the unequal distribution of sociodemographic  subgroups  may  result  in  algorithm  bias  and serve to widen known disparities in breast cancer

mortality. 7,8 By  investigating  bias  before  algorithm  deployment, teams can take steps to mitigate it. 28

We investigated potential algorithmic bias across registries, racial  categories,  Hispanic  Ethnicity,  age  at  diagnosis,  and SES using traditional ML metrics and 4 bias metrics: BER, DP, EOD, and equalized opportunity (EOP). Each metric utilizes a different aspect of algorithmic results. The BER score highlighted misclassification rates of cancer reports, particularly for the rare triple-negative (TNBC) class. The DP score measured  the  ratio  of  positive  classifications  across  demographics,  ensuring  no  group  was  deprioritized.  The  EOD score  compared  correct  and  incorrect  classifications  across demographics  to  assess  for  differences  in  underrepresented groups. Finally, the EOP score evaluated the overall accuracy of cancer report classifications, ensuring all patients receive the necessary attention. Employing a variety of bias metrics provided  a  comprehensive  view  of  model  performance  and evaluated fairness.

Socioeconomic factors significantly  impact  survival  rates, with lower income and less education linked to decreased survival rates. 7,8 Clinical models trained by using data only from affluent institutions may  misclassify e-paths from lowresource institutions, perpetuating SES disparities. However, no significant bias in extracting biomarker data toward SES for  any  metric.  While  BER  results  revealed  significant  bias toward the Seattle registry, the other metrics did not. The differences in registries may stem from differences in the laboratory practices and interpretation guidelines across institutions and healthcare systems. The model does not explicitly learn socioeconomic features, which might be an effect due to the standardization of information that SEER implements when reporting e-paths; all registrars extract the same information from e-paths regardless of the original institution.

The American Cancer Society reports higher breast cancer incidence rates for Black women in States: Alabama, Louisiana, Mississippi, and Virginia, with Kentucky also showing elevated  rates,  although  they  were  not  statistically  significant. 3 Evaluating racial differences for women in each registry showed no BER bias toward the White majority, except in New Mexico, which exhibited a significant bias toward the Black  racial  category.  Additionally,  the  model  performed well  on  TNBC  tumors  across  most  registries,  except  New Mexico  and  Utah.  This  demonstrates  that  the  model  can accurately discern tumor classes for non-TNBC tumors.

Investigating data bias is a crucial in ML applications given age  and  racial  differences.  While  looking  at  incidence  and mortality rates, in the United States, breast cancer incidence rates  are  highest  among  White  women,  followed  by  Black, Native  American,  Asian  and  Pacific  Islander,  and  Hispanic women. 3 Mortality rates are highest among Black and Native American women. Unequal data distribution in clinical ML may compromise overall model accuracy, potentially exacerbating health inequalities in these populations. However, the results showed no significant bias toward the majority class in  terms  of  age,  race,  and  Hispanic  ethnicity  indicating  the model  did  not  learn  these  features  likely  due  to  pathology reports  focusing  on  cell  tissue  rather  than  demographics. 29 Further studies should examine the algorithmic bias of Hispanic women with TNBC. 30,31

Despite  using  multiple  bias  metrics,  we  only  found  bias when using the BER score, which corresponds to low recall scores for each subgroup. Low recall scores indicate a higher number of false negatives, and when the recall score for the majority class was much greater than the minority, the BER score (the average of error rates for the positive and negative classes)  captured  the  increased  misclassification  rates.  The higher BER suggests that the model's difficulty in correctly identifying positive cases, seen in registry-level data, creates a bias that is not evident in the other metrics. This underscores the importance of using multiple metrics to measure model performance.

A potential data quality issue is that SEER may have historically used patients' last names as a proxy for race, though new rules  prohibit  this  from  2011  forward. 21,32 Given our recent  data,  we  believe  it  should  exhibit  as  minimal  bias. Another  limitation  is  implicit  bias,  or  unconscious  bias, which may have been introduced in earlier phases of diagnosing breast cancer: We have no method to measure this at the registry level. 33,34 The  authors acknowledge  that using county-level Median Family Income as a proxy for socioeconomic  status  may  obscure  individual-level  socioeconomic effects, as there can be substantial individual level heterogeneity within counties.

Bias metrics should be integrated into clinical applications to  assess  model  performance,  with  metrics  tailored  to  each clinical  context.  As  ML  applications  integrate  into  clinical settings,  reporting  bias  metrics  increases  transparency  and leads to more robust, fair, and reliable models. The acceptable error rate should be standardized across healthcare institutions to ensure equal treatment for all patients facilitating the  scaling  of  models  and  ultimately  enhancing  the  quality and equity of the healthcare system.

The Department of Energy (DOE) and the National Cancer Institute (NCI) have a collaborative partnership that has been actively  utilizing  the  models  used  in  this  study  for  cancer information  extraction  in  the  SEER  registries  for  approximately 2 years. 35 While the architecture is different than large language models (LLMs), the performance is comparable (see Table 3 36 ). The HiSAN model has a built-in abstention mechanism, that identifies reports that require human annotator review. The model is tuned to autocode records that surpass the 97% confidence threshold and abstain on any uncertain predictions.

We recognize that LLMs, such as GPT-4, offer promising potential for improving performance in information extraction tasks. 36 And we are working to test our current implementation an LLM for national scale deployment. We plan to extend the methods presented in this paper in future work.

This study highlights the importance of evaluating and mitigating bias before deployment deep learning models. Moving forward, it will be important to assess how next-generation models  can  integrate  into  clinical  settings,  ensuring  that improvements in performance do not come at the cost of fairness or transparency.

# Conclusion

This  study  investigated  algorithmic  bias  perpetuated  from representation bias in the data using ML architectures provided by the FrESCO framework across 5 subgroups-registry,  racial  categories,  Hispanic  ethnicity,  age  at  diagnosis, and SES-from 6 SEER registries. The objective was to discern  disparities  arising  from  imbalanced  data  and  their impact on model performance. We quantified bias using F1 scores, precision, BER, DP, EOD, and EOP uncovering features  the  model  relies  on  for  classification.  Without  bias

metrics, unrepresented groups could be further marginalized in healthcare settings. Future work will focus on mitigating bias toward majority groups for equitable performance of all minority classes.

# Acknowledgments

The authors would like to acknowledge the contribution to this study from other staff in the participating central cancer registries. These registries are supported by the NCI's SEER program, the  Centers  for  Disease  Control  and  Prevention's National Program of Cancer Registries (NPCR), and/or state agencies,  universities,  and  cancer  centers.  The  participating central cancer registries include the following:

Public Health Institute, SEER HHSN261201800009I

Kentucky Cancer Registry, SEER HHSN261201800013I and HHSN26100001 and NPCR NU58DP003907

Louisiana Tumor Registry, SEER HHSN261201800007I and HHSN26100002 and NPCR NU58DP0063.

New Jersey State Cancer Registry, SEER 75N91021D 0000 and 75N91021F00001 and NPCR NU58DP006279

New  Mexico  Tumor  Registry,  SEER  HHSN261601 800014l

Fred  Hutchinson  Cancer  Center,  SEER  HHSN26120 18000041

Utah  Cancer  Registry,  SEER  HHSN261201800016I and NPCR NU58DP007131

Jordan Tschida (Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Investigation,  Methodology,  Writing-original  draft,  Writingreview & editing), Mayanka Chandrashekar (Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing-review & editing), Alina Peluso (Writing-review & editing), Zachary Fox (Writing-review &  editing),  Patrycja  Krawczuk  (Data  curation,  Software, Writing-review  &  editing),  Dakota  Murdock  (Data  curation, Software, Writing-review & editing), Xiao-Cheng Wu (Data  curation,  Writing-review  &  editing),  John  Gounley (Software, Writing-review & editing), and Heidi A. Hanson (Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing-review & editing)

# Supplementary material

Supplementary material is available at JAMIA Open online.

# Funding

This work has been supported in part by the US Department of  Energy  (DOE)  and  the  National  Cancer  Institute  of  the National  Institutes  of  Health.  This  work  was  performed under the auspices of the DOE by Argonne National Laboratory under Contract DE-AC02-06-CH11357, Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory under Contract  DEAC5207NA27344, Los Alamos National Laboratory under Contract DE-AC5206NA25396, and Oak Ridge National Laboratory under Contract DE-AC05-00OR22725.

# Conflicts of interest

The authors have none to declare.

# Data availability

The data used for our experiments consist of cancer pathology  reports  that  potentially  contain  identifiers  as  defined under HIPAA and would be protected health information; as such,  we  are  not  authorized  to  make  our  dataset  publicly available.  These  data  were  acquired  and  used  under  an approved IRB protocol and data use agreement with the data owners-the NCI's SEER cancer registries in Kentucky, Louisiana,  New  Mexico,  New  Jersey,  Seattle,  and  Utah.  These documents stipulate the specific activities for which we have been authorized to use this data. De-identification with the intent to distribute publicly is not an authorized use of the data based on these agreements. The data owner imposes this in agreement with the data user. The data used in our experiments may be accessible upon request and with subsequent authorized approvals by contacting each SEER Registry. 37

# Notice

Office  of  Science  of  the  US  Department  of  Energy.  This manuscript  has  been  authored  by  UT-Battelle,  LLC,  under contract DE-AC05-00OR22725 with the US Department of Energy (DOE). The US government retains and the publisher, by  accepting  the  article  for  publication,  acknowledges  that the US government retains a nonexclusive, paid-up, irrevocable, worldwide license to publish or reproduce the published form of this manuscript, or allow others to do so, for US government purposes. DOE will provide public access to these results of federally sponsored research in accordance with the DOE  Public  Access  Plan  (http://energy.gov/downloads/doepublic-access-plan).
